title: Transformation of Corporate Scope in U.S. Banks: Patterns and Performance Implications

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This staff report presents the first population-wide study of business scope transformation in U.S. banks using a novel database of bank holding company organizational structures. The research finds that expanding scope has a negative impact on performance on average, but firms whose expansion keeps them closer to the prevailing 'modal bank' fare better than those pursuing generic diversification. Early expanders into particular activities benefit more, while late adopters lose out rather than benefiting by fitting the norm.
key_points: Authors: Nicola Cetorelli, Michael G. Jacobides, and Samuel Stern; published as Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report No. 813, May 2017
First population-wide study examining transformation in business scope of U.S. bank holding companies using a novel database of organizational structure details
Expanding scope has a negative impact on performance on average for U.S. banks
Firms whose expansion keeps them closer to the prevailing 'modal bank' outperform those pursuing generic diversification
Early expanders into particular activities benefit more from diversification than late adopters
Late adopters do not benefit from 'fitting the norm' and instead lose out compared to earlier movers
JEL classification: G21, L22, L25; paper available only in PDF format (52 pages, 847 kb)
why_it_matters: This research provides empirical evidence on how bank organizational structure affects performance, relevant for bank management strategy, regulatory oversight, and understanding competitive dynamics in the banking sector. The finding that generic diversification destroys value while disciplined expansion near industry norms creates value has implications for how banks structure their business lines.
market_structure_context: This paper directly addresses market structure by examining bank holding company organizational structure and business scope. It studies how the breadth of activities (corporate scope) affects bank performance across the population of U.S. banks, using time-series details of organizational structure.
